source_anchors.title_authors_venue: ares_rag_evaluation_source_evidence.md E01 identifies the title, authors, venue, and year.
source_anchors.method_and_dimensions: ares_rag_evaluation_source_evidence.md E02 identifies context relevance, answer faithfulness, answer relevance, synthetic training data, lightweight LM judges, and PPI.
source_anchors.evaluation_scope_and_results: ares_rag_evaluation_source_evidence.md E03 identifies KILT/SuperGLUE/AIS scope and ranking-accuracy comparisons with RAGAS/GPT-3.5.
source_anchors.limitations: ares_rag_evaluation_source_evidence.md E04 describes the small validation set, domain expertise, GPU resource, and English-dataset limitations.

title: ARES: An Automated Evaluation Framework for Retrieval-Augmented Generation Systems
authors_display: Jon Saad-Falcon, Omar Khattab, Christopher Potts, and Matei Zaharia
publication: Proceedings of NAACL-HLT 2024, Volume 1: Long Papers
main_task: Automated evaluation of RAG systems by creating tailored LM judges for context relevance, answer faithfulness, and answer relevance.
main_metric: Context relevance, answer faithfulness, and answer relevance scores, with prediction-powered inference confidence intervals.
method: ARES creates synthetic training data from in-domain passages, fine-tunes lightweight LM judges, and uses a small human preference validation set with prediction-powered inference.
evaluation_scope: Eight knowledge-intensive datasets from KILT, SuperGLUE, and AIS.
key_result: ARES beats existing automated approaches such as RAGAS on context relevance and answer relevance evaluation accuracy, and uses far fewer annotations than annotation-based evaluation.
limitation: ARES still needs a small human preference validation set, may require domain-specialist annotators for law/medicine/finance, needs substantial GPU resources for the judge and generator models, and was evaluated only on English datasets.
